title: The Federalist Papers
author: Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, John Jay

anchors.question: Separate Madison's stated position (the necessity of a republican remedy) from the underlying interests he ascribes to (a) propertied minorities and (b) the unpropertied majority. Identify which interests he treats as structurally permanent versus contingent.
anchors.expected_subgraph_summary: Two interest classes attached to two actor roles, with explicit constraint edges to property as a structural cause of faction; Madison's position cited; the republican-remedy claim linked to the constraint of size.

anchors.section: FEDERALIST No. 70 (Hamilton) — Unity in the Executive
anchors.question: Map Hamilton's argument as a typed graph: which constraint shapes are claimed to require executive unity? What leverage mechanisms does he attribute to a unitary executive? Surface the implicit counter-position he is arguing against.
anchors.expected_subgraph_summary: A unitary-executive actor with three claimed leverage mechanisms (energy, secrecy, dispatch); an implicit Anti-Federalist counter-actor with a claim of guarded-against tyranny; two constraint edges linking republican accountability to executive unity.

anchors.question: Hamilton famously calls the judiciary 'the least dangerous branch.' Is that a Claim, an Interest, or a Commitment in the ACO sense? Track the typed inference chain from the structural feature he cites (no will, only judgment) to the political conclusion.
anchors.expected_subgraph_summary: Claim with a specific modality; constraint edge from 'no will, only judgment' to a leverage-deficit edge; commitment by ratifying actors implicit; the inference chain explicit and source-bound.

notes: Edition-stable since 1788. The Gutenberg edition uses standard paper numbering. Character offsets are robust across most printings; the canonical paper boundaries are stable. Anti-Federalist counter-positions (Brutus, Cato) are not in this manifest — see `corpora/public-domain/anti-federalist.json` (planned).
